1.anchor: For every high priest is taken from among the people
1.note: tn Grk “from among men,” but since the point in context is shared humanity (rather than shared maleness), the plural Greek term ἀνθρώπων (anqrwpwn) has been translated “people.”
tn Grk “who is taken from among people is appointed.”
tn Grk “appointed on behalf of people in reference to things relating to God.”

4.anchor: And no one assumes this honor
4.note: sn Honor refers here to the honor of the high priesthood.
tn Grk “by himself, on his own.”

4.anchor: but only when called to it by God,

5.anchor: So also Christ did not glorify himself in becoming high priest, but the one who glorified him was God,
5.note: tn Grk “the one”; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
tn Grk “I have begotten you”; see Heb 1:5.sn A quotation from Ps 2:7.

6.anchor: as also in another place God
6.note: tn Grk “he”; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
sn A quotation from Ps 110:4.

7.note: tn Grk “in the days of his flesh.”
tn Grk “he”; the referent (Christ) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
tn Grk “who…having offered,” continuing the description of Christ from Heb 5:5-6.

8.anchor: Although he was a son, he learned obedience through the things he suffered.
8.note: sn There is a wordplay in the Greek text between the verbs “learned” (ἔμαθεν, emaqen) and “suffered” (ἔπαθεν, epaqen).

10.note: tn Grk “having been designated,” continuing the thought of Heb 5:9.
sn The phrase in the order of Melchizedek picks up the quotation from Ps 110:4 in Heb 5:6.

11.anchor: On this topic we have much to say
11.note: tn Grk “concerning which the message for us is great.”

11.anchor: and it is difficult to explain, since you have become sluggish

12.anchor: For though you should in fact be teachers by this time,
12.note: tn Grk “because of the time.”

12.anchor: you need someone to teach you the beginning elements of God’s utterances.
12.note: tn Grk “the elements of the beginning of the oracles of God.”

12.anchor: You have gone back to needing
12.note: tn Grk “you have come to have a need for.”

tc ‡ Most texts, including some early and important ones (א2 A B* D Ψ 0122 0278 1881 Ï sy Cl), have καί (kai, “and”) immediately preceding οὐ (ou, “not”), but other equally significant witnesses (Ì46 א* B2 C 33 81 1739 lat Or Did) lack the conjunction. As it was a natural tendency for scribes to add a coordinating conjunction, the καί appears to be a motivated reading. On balance, it is probably best to regard the shorter reading as authentic. NA27 has καί in brackets, indicating doubts as to its authenticity.

1.note: tn Grk “let us fear.”

2.anchor: For we had good news proclaimed to us just as they did. But the message they heard did them no good, since they did not join in
2.note: tn Or “they were not united.”

2.anchor: with those who heard it in faith.
2.note: tc A few mss (א and a few versional witnesses) have the nominative singular participle συγκεκερασμένος (sunkekerasmeno", “since it [the message] was not combined with faith by those who heard it”), a reading that refers back to the ὁ λόγος (Jo logo", “the message”). There are a few other variants here (e.g., συγκεκεραμμένοι [sunkekerammenoi] in 104, συγκεκεραμένους [sunkekeramenou"] in 1881 Ï), but the accusative plural participle συγκεκερασμένους (sunkekerasmenou"), found in Ì13vid,46 A B C D* Ψ 0243 0278 33 81 1739 2464 pc, has by far the best external credentials. This participle agrees with the previous ἐκείνους (ekeinou", “those”), a more difficult construction grammatically than the nominative singular. Thus, both on external and internal grounds, συγκεκερασμένους is preferred.

3.note: tn Grk “although the works,” continuing the previous reference to God. The referent (God) is specified in the translation for clarity.

5.anchor: but to repeat the text cited earlier:
5.note: tn Grk “and in this again.”

7.note: tn Grk “he”; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

7.anchor: again ordains a certain day, “Today,” speaking through David
7.note: sn Ps 95 does not mention David either in the text or the superscription. It is possible that the writer of Hebrews is attributing the entire collection of psalms to David (although some psalms are specifically attributed to other individuals or groups).

7.anchor: after so long a time, as in the words quoted before,
7.note: tn Grk “as it has been said before” (see Heb 3:7).

7.anchor: that today you would listen as he speaks!
7.note: tn Grk “today if you hear his voice.”

8.anchor: For if Joshua had given them rest, God
8.note: tn Grk “he”; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

10.anchor: For the one who enters God’s
10.note: tn Grk “his”; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

13.anchor: And no creature is hidden from God,
13.note: tn Grk “him”; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

16.anchor: Therefore let us confidently approach the throne of grace to receive mercy and find grace whenever we need help.

1.anchor: For the law possesses a shadow of the good things to come but not the reality itself, and is therefore completely unable, by the same sacrifices offered continually, year after year, to perfect those who come to worship.
1.note: tn Grk “those who approach.”

2.anchor: For otherwise would they not have ceased to be offered, since the worshipers would have been purified once for all and so have
2.note: tn Grk “the worshipers, having been purified once for all, would have.”

3.note: tn Grk “in them”; the referent (those sacrifices) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

4.anchor: For the blood of bulls and goats cannot take away sins.
4.note: tn Grk “for it is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins.”

7.note: tn Grk “behold,” but this construction often means “here is/there is” (cf. BDAG 468 s.v. ἰδού 2).
sn A quotation from Ps 40:6-8 (LXX). The phrase a body you prepared for me (in v. 5) is apparently an interpretive expansion of the HT reading “ears you have dug out for me.”

8.note: sn Various phrases from the quotation of Ps 40:6 in Heb 10:5-6 are repeated in Heb 10:8.

9.note: tc The majority of mss, especially the later ones (א2 0278vid 1739 Ï lat), have ὁ θεός (Jo qeo", “God”) at this point, while most of the earliest and best witnesses lack such an explicit addressee (so Ì46 א* A C D K P Ψ 33 1175 1881 2464 al). The longer reading is a palpable corruption, apparently motivated in part by the wording of Ps 40:8 (39:9 LXX) and by the word order of this same verse as quoted in Heb 10:7.

10.note: tn Grk “by which will.” Because of the length and complexity of the Greek sentence, a new sentence was started here in the translation.

11.anchor: And every priest stands day after day

12.note: tn Grk “this one.” This pronoun refers to Jesus, but “this priest” was used in the translation to make the contrast between the Jewish priests in v. 11 and Jesus as a priest clearer in English.

12.anchor: he sat down at the right hand
12.note: sn An allusion to Ps 110:1.

15.anchor: And the Holy Spirit also witnesses to us, for after saying,
15.note: tn Grk “after having said,” emphasizing the present impact of this utterance.

19.note: tn Grk “brothers.” See note on the phrase “brothers and sisters” in 2:11.

20.anchor: by the fresh and living way that he inaugurated for us
20.note: tn Grk “that he inaugurated for us as a fresh and living way,” referring to the entrance mentioned in v. 19.

20.anchor: through the curtain, that is, through his flesh,
20.note: sn Through his flesh. In a bold shift the writer changes from a spatial phrase (Christ opened the way through the curtain into the inner sanctuary) to an instrumental phrase (he did this through [by means of] his flesh in his sacrifice of himself), associating the two in an allusion to the splitting of the curtain in the temple from top to bottom (Matt 27:51; Mark 15:38; Luke 23:45). Just as the curtain was split, so Christ’s body was broken for us, to give us access into God’s presence.

21.anchor: and since we have a great priest
21.note: tn Grk “and a great priest,” continuing the construction begun in v. 19.

22.anchor: let us draw near with a sincere heart in the assurance that faith brings,
because we have had our hearts sprinkled clean from an evil conscience
22.note: sn The phrase our hearts sprinkled clean from an evil conscience combines the OT imagery of the sprinkling with blood to give ritual purity with the emphasis on the interior cleansing provided by the new covenant: It is the heart that is cleansed and the conscience made perfect (cf. Heb 8:10; 9:9, 14; 10:2, 16).

24.anchor: And let us take thought of how to spur one another on to love and good works,
24.note: tn Grk “let us consider one another for provoking of love and good deeds.”

25.anchor: not abandoning our own meetings, as some are in the habit of doing, but encouraging each other, and even more so because you see the day
25.note: sn The day refers to that well-known time of Christ’s coming and judgment in the future; see a similar use of “day” in 1 Cor 3:13.

tn This paragraph (vv. 19-25) is actually a single, skillfully composed sentence in Greek, but it must be broken into shorter segments for English idiom. It begins with several subordinate phrases (since we have confidence and a great priest), has three parallel exhortations as its main verbs (let us draw near, hold, and take thought), and concludes with several subordinate phrases related to the final exhortation (not abandoning but encouraging).

26.anchor: For if we deliberately keep on sinning after receiving the knowledge of the truth, no further sacrifice for sins is left for us,
26.note: tn Grk “is left,” with “for us” implied by the first half of the verse.

27.note: tn Grk “zeal,” recalling God’s jealous protection of his holiness and honor (cf. Exod 20:5).sn An allusion to Zeph 1:18.
tn Grk “the enemies.”sn An allusion to Isa 26:11.

28.anchor: Someone who rejected the law of Moses was put to death

29.anchor: How much greater punishment do you think that person deserves who has contempt for
the Son of God, and profanes
the blood of the covenant that made him holy,
29.note: tn Grk “by which he was made holy.”

34.anchor: For in fact you shared the sufferings of those in prison,
34.note: tc Most witnesses, including some important ones (א D2 1881 Ï), read δεσμοῖς μου (desmoi" mou, “my imprisonment”) here, a reading that is probably due to the widespread belief in the early Christian centuries that Paul was the author of Hebrews (cf. Phil 1:7; Col 4:18). It may have been generated by the reading δεσμοῖς without the μου (so Ì46 Ψ 104 pc), the force of which is so ambiguous (lit., “you shared the sufferings with the bonds”) as to be virtually nonsensical. Most likely, δεσμοῖς resulted when a scribe made an error in copying δεσμίοις (desmioi"), a reading which makes excellent sense (“[of] those in prison”) and is strongly supported by early and significant witnesses of the Alexandrian and Western texttypes (A D* H 6 33 81 1739 lat sy co). Thus, δεσμίοις best explains the rise of the other readings on both internal and external grounds and is strongly preferred.

34.anchor: and you accepted the confiscation of your belongings with joy, because you knew that you certainly

35.anchor: So do not throw away your confidence, because it
35.note: tn Grk “which,” but showing the reason.

36.anchor: For you need endurance in order to do God’s will and so receive what is promised.
36.note: tn Grk “the promise,” referring to the thing God promised, not to the pledge itself.

39.anchor: But we are not among those who shrink back and thus perish, but are among those who have faith and preserve their souls.
39.note: tn Grk “not…of shrinking back to perdition but of faith to the preservation of the soul.”

1.note: tn Grk “the first” (referring to the covenant described in Heb 8:7, 13). In the translation the referent (covenant) has been specified for clarity.

2.anchor: For a tent was prepared, the outer one,
2.note: tn Grk “the first,” in order of approach in the ritual.
tn Grk “in which [were].”

2.anchor: the lampstand, the table, and the presentation of the loaves; this
2.note: tn Grk “which,” describing the outer tent.

4.anchor: It contained the golden altar of incense and the ark of the covenant covered entirely with gold. In this ark
4.note: tn Grk “in which”; in the translation the referent (the ark) has been specified for clarity.

5.note: tn Grk “above it”; in the translation the referent (the ark) has been specified for clarity.

sn The cherubim (pl.) were an order of angels mentioned repeatedly in the OT but only here in the NT. They were associated with God’s presence, glory, and holiness. Their images that sat on top of the ark of the covenant are described in Exod 25:18-20.

6.anchor: So with these things prepared like this, the priests enter continually into the outer tent
6.note: tn Grk “the first tent.”

7.anchor: But only the high priest enters once a year into the inner tent,
7.note: tn Grk “the second tent.”

7.anchor: and not without blood that he offers for himself and for the sins of the people committed in ignorance.
7.note: tn Or perhaps “the unintentional sins of the people”; Grk “the ignorances of the people.” Cf. BDAG 13 s.v. ἀγνόημα, “sin committed in ignorance/unintentionally.” This term seems to be simply a synonym for “sins” (cf. Heb 5:2) and does not pick up the distinction made in Num 15:22-31 between unwitting sin and “high-handed” sin. The Day of Atonement ritual in Lev 16 covered all the sins of the people, not just the unwitting ones.

8.anchor: The Holy Spirit is making clear that the way into the holy place had not yet appeared as long as the old tabernacle
8.note: tn Grk “the first tent.” The literal phrase “the first tent” refers to either (1) the outer chamber of the tabernacle in the wilderness (as in vv. 2, 6) or (2) the entire tabernacle as a symbol of the OT system of approaching God. The second is more likely given the contrast that follows in vv. 11-12.

10.anchor: They served only for matters of food and drink
10.note: tn Grk “only for foods and drinks.”

10.anchor: and various washings; they are external regulations
10.note: tc Most witnesses (D1 Ï) have “various washings, and external regulations” (βαπτισμοῖς καὶ δικαιώμασιν, baptismoi" kai dikaiwmasin), with both nouns in the dative. The translation “washings; they are… regulations” renders βαπτισμοῖς, δικαιώματα (baptismoi", dikaiwmata; found in such important mss as Ì46 א* A I P 0278 33 1739 1881 al sa) in which case δικαιώματα is taken as the nominative subject of the participle ἐπικείμενα (epikeimena). It seems far more likely that scribes would conform δικαιώματα to the immediately preceding datives and join it to them by καί than they would to the following nominative participle. Both on external and internal evidence the text is thus secure as reading βαπτισμοῖς, δικαιώματα.

10.anchor: imposed until the new order came.
10.note: tn Grk “until the time of setting things right.”

11.note: tn Grk “But Christ, when he came,” introducing a sentence that includes all of Heb 9:11-12. The main construction is “Christ, having come…, entered…, having secured…,” and everything else describes his entrance.

12.anchor: and he entered once for all into the most holy place not by the blood of goats and calves but by his own blood, and so he himself secured
12.note: tn This verb occurs in the Greek middle voice, which here intensifies the role of the subject, Christ, in accomplishing the action: “he alone secured”; “he and no other secured.”

13.anchor: For if the blood of goats and bulls and the ashes of a young cow sprinkled on those who are defiled consecrated them and provided ritual purity,
13.note: tn Grk “for the purifying of the flesh.” The “flesh” here is symbolic of outward or ritual purity in contrast to inner purity, that of the conscience (cf. Heb 9:9).

14.anchor: how much more will the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God, purify our
14.note: tc The reading adopted by the translation is attested by many authorities (A D* K P 365 1739* al). But many others (א D2 0278 33 1739c 1881 Ï lat sa) read “your” instead of “our.” The diversity of evidence makes this a difficult case to decide from external evidence alone. The first and second person pronouns differ by only one letter in Greek, as in English, also making this problem difficult to decide based on internal evidence and transcriptional probability. In the context, the author’s description of sacrificial activities seems to invite the reader to compare his own possible participation in OT liturgy as over against the completed work of Christ, so the second person pronoun “your” might make more sense. On the other hand, TCGNT 599 argues that “our” is preferable because the author of Hebrews uses direct address (i.e., the second person) only in the hortatory sections. What is more, the author seems to prefer the first person in explanatory remarks or when giving the logical grounds for an assertion (cf. Heb 4:15; 7:14). It is hard to reach a definitive conclusion in this case, but the data lean slightly in favor of the first person pronoun.

15.anchor: And so he is the mediator
15.note: tn The Greek word μεσίτης (mesith", “mediator”) in this context does not imply that Jesus was a mediator in the contemporary sense of the word, i.e., he worked for compromise between opposing parties. Here the term describes his function as the one who was used by God to enact a new covenant which established a new relationship between God and his people, but entirely on God’s terms.

15.anchor: of a new covenant, so that those who are called may receive the eternal inheritance he has promised,
15.note: tn Grk “the promise of the eternal inheritance.”

16.anchor: For where there is a will, the death of the one who made it must be proven.
16.note: tn Grk “there is a necessity for the death of the one who made it to be proven.”

18.anchor: So even the first covenant was inaugurated with blood.
18.note: sn The Greek text reinforces this by negating the opposite (“not even the first covenant was inaugurated without blood”), but this double negation is not used in contemporary English.

20.note: tn Grk “which God commanded for you (or in your case).”sn A quotation from Exod 24:8.

23.anchor: So it was necessary for the sketches
23.note: tn Or “prototypes,” “outlines,” referring to the earthly sanctuary. See Heb 8:5 above for the prior use of this term.

23.anchor: of the things in heaven to be purified with these sacrifices,
23.note: tn Grk “with these”; in the translation the referent (sacrifices) has been specified for clarity.

23.anchor: but the heavenly things themselves required
23.note: tn Grk “the heavenly things themselves with better sacrifices than these.”

24.anchor: For Christ did not enter a sanctuary made with hands – the representation

24.note: tn The word “sanctuary” is not in the Greek text at this point, but has been supplied for clarity.

25.anchor: And he did not enter to offer
25.note: tn Grk “and not that he might offer,” continuing the previous construction.

27.note: tn Here ἀνθρώποις (anqrwpoi") has been translated as a generic noun (“people”).

27.anchor: are appointed to die once, and then to face judgment,

28.anchor: to those who eagerly await him he will appear a second time, not to bear sin
28.note: tn Grk “without sin,” but in context this does not refer to Christ’s sinlessness (as in Heb 4:15) but to the fact that sin is already dealt with by his first coming.
tn Grk “for salvation.” This may be construed with the verb “await” (those who wait for him to bring them salvation), but the connection with “appear” (as in the translation) is more likely.

2.anchor: For if the message spoken through angels
2.note: sn The message spoken through angels refers to the OT law, which according to Jewish tradition was mediated to Moses through angels (cf. Deut 33:2; Ps 68:17-18; Acts 7:38, 53; Gal 3:19; and Jub. 1:27, 29; Josephus, Ant. 15.5.3 [15.136]).

2.anchor: proved to be so firm that every violation
2.note: tn Grk “through angels became valid and every violation.”

4.note: tn Grk “God bearing witness together” (the phrase “with them” is implied).

4.anchor: with signs and wonders and various miracles and gifts of the Holy Spirit distributed
4.note: tn Grk “and distributions of the Holy Spirit.”

5.anchor: For he did not put the world to come,
5.note: sn The phrase the world to come means “the coming inhabited earth,” using the Greek term which describes the world of people and their civilizations.
sn See the previous reference to the world in Heb 1:6.

6.anchor: What is man that you think of him
6.note: tn Grk “remember him.”

7.note: tc Several witnesses, many of them early and important (א A C D* P Ψ 0243 0278 33 1739 1881 al lat co), have at the end of v 7, “You have given him dominion over the works of your hands.” Other mss, not quite as impressive in weight, lack the words (Ì46 B D2 Ï). In spite of the impressive external evidence for the longer reading, it is most likely a scribal addition to conform the text of Hebrews to Ps 8:6 (8:7 LXX). Conformity of a NT quotation of the OT to the LXX was a routine scribal activity, and can hardly be in doubt here as to the cause of the longer reading.

8.note: sn The expression all things under his control occurs three times in 2:8. The latter two occurrences are not exactly identical to the Greek text of Ps 8:6 quoted at the beginning of the verse, but have been adapted by the writer of Hebrews to fit his argument.

9.note: tn Or “who was made a little lower than the angels.”

9.anchor: now crowned with glory and honor because he suffered death,
9.note: tn Grk “because of the suffering of death.”

9.anchor: so that by God’s grace he would experience
9.note: tn Grk “would taste.” Here the Greek verb does not mean “sample a small amount” (as a typical English reader might infer from the word “taste”), but “experience something cognitively or emotionally; come to know something” (cf. BDAG 195 s.v. γεύομαι 2).

10.anchor: For it was fitting for him, for whom and through whom all things exist,
10.note: tn Grk “for whom are all things and through whom are all things.”

10.anchor: in bringing many sons to glory, to make the pioneer
10.note: sn The Greek word translated pioneer is used of a “prince” or leader, the representative head of a family. It also carries nuances of “trailblazer,” one who breaks through to new ground for those who follow him. It is used some thirty-five times in the Greek OT and four times in the NT, always of Christ (Acts 3:15; 5:31; Heb 2:10; 12:2).

11.anchor: For indeed he who makes holy and those being made holy all have the same origin,
he is not ashamed to call them brothers and sisters,
11.note: tn Grk “brothers,” but the Greek word may be used for “brothers and sisters” as here (cf. BDAG 18 s.v. ἀδελφός 1, where considerable nonbiblical evidence for the plural ἀδελφοί [adelfoi] meaning “brothers and sisters” is cited). The context here also indicates both men and women are in view; note especially the collective τὰ παιδία (ta paidia) in v. 14.

12.anchor: I will proclaim your name to my brothers;
12.note: tn Here, because of its occurrence in an OT quotation, τοῖς ἀδελφοῖς (tois adelfois) has been translated simply as “brothers” rather than “brothers and sisters” (see the note on the latter phrase in the previous verse).
sn A quotation from Ps 22:22.

13.note: tn Grk “and again,” as a continuation of the preceding.
tn Grk “behold, I,” but this construction often means “here is/there is” (cf. BDAG 468 s.v. ἰδού 2).

14.anchor: Therefore, since the children share in flesh and blood, he likewise shared in
14.note: tn Or “partook of” (this is a different word than the one in v. 14a).

14.anchor: so that through death he could destroy
14.note: tn Or “break the power of,” “reduce to nothing.”

17.anchor: to be made like his brothers and sisters
17.note: tn Grk “brothers.” See note on the phrase “brothers and sisters” in 2:11.

17.anchor: in every respect, so that he could become a merciful and faithful high priest in things relating to God, to make atonement

1.note: tn Grk “Therefore leaving behind.” The implication is not of abandoning this elementary information, but of building on it.
tn Or “basic.”
tn Grk “the message of the beginning of Christ.”
tn Grk “leaving behind…let us move on.”

3.anchor: And this is what we intend to do,
3.note: tn Grk “and we will do this.”

6.anchor: and then have committed apostasy,
6.note: tn Or “have fallen away.”

6.anchor: to renew them again to repentance, since
6.note: tn Or “while”; Grk “crucifying…and holding.” The Greek participles here (“crucifying…and holding”) can be understood as either causal (“since”) or temporal (“while”).

6.anchor: they are crucifying the Son of God for themselves all over again
6.note: tn Grk “recrucifying the son of God for themselves.”

7.anchor: For the ground that has soaked up the rain that frequently falls on
7.note: tn Grk “comes upon.”

8.anchor: But if it produces thorns and thistles, it is useless and about to be cursed;

12.anchor: so that you may not be sluggish,
12.note: tn Or “dull.”

14.note: tn Grk “in blessing I will bless you and in multiplying I will multiply you,” the Greek form of a Hebrew idiom showing intensity.sn A quotation from Gen 22:17.

15.note: tn Grk “he”; in the translation the referent (Abraham) has been specified for clarity.

16.note: tn The plural Greek term ἄνθρωποι (anqrwpoi) is used here in a generic sense, referring to both men and women, and is thus translated “people.”

16.anchor: swear by something greater than themselves,
16.note: tn Grk “by something greater”; the rest of the comparison (“than themselves”) is implied.

16.anchor: and the oath serves as a confirmation to end all dispute.
16.note: tn Grk “the oath for confirmation is an end of all dispute.”

17.anchor: God wanted to demonstrate more clearly to the heirs of the promise that his purpose was unchangeable,
17.note: tn Or “immutable” (here and in v. 18); Grk “the unchangeableness of his purpose.”

18.anchor: so that we who have found refuge in him
18.note: tn Grk “have taken refuge”; the basis of that refuge is implied in the preceding verse.

19.anchor: We have this hope as an anchor for the soul, sure and steadfast, which reaches inside behind the curtain,
19.note: sn The curtain refers to the veil or drape in the temple that separated the holy place from the holy of holies.

20.note: sn A quotation from Ps 110:4, picked up again from Heb 5:6, 10.

1.note: tn Grk “brothers.” See note on the phrase “brothers and sisters” in 2:11.

1.anchor: partners in a heavenly calling, take note of Jesus, the apostle and high priest whom we confess,
1.note: tn Grk “of our confession.”

2.anchor: who is faithful to the one who appointed him, as Moses was also in God’s
2.note: tn Grk “his”; in the translation the referent (God) has been specified for clarity.

tc ‡ The reading adopted by the translation follows a few early mss and some versions (Ì13,46vid B vgms co Ambr). The majority of mss (א A C D Ψ 0243 0278 33 1739 1881 Ï lat sy) insert “all” (“in all his house”), apparently in anticipation of Heb 3:5 which quotes directly from Num 12:7. On balance, the omission better explains the rise of ὅλῳ ({olw, “all”) than vice versa. NA27 puts ὅλῳ in brackets, indicating doubts as to its authenticity.

5.note: tn Grk “his”; in the translation the referent (God) has been specified for clarity.
sn A quotation from Num 12:7.

6.anchor: But Christ
6.note: sn The Greek makes the contrast between v. 5 and v. 6a more emphatic and explicit than is easily done in English.

6.anchor: is faithful as a son over God’s
6.note: tn Grk “his”; in the translation the referent (God) has been specified for clarity.

6.anchor: house. We are of his house,
6.note: tn Grk “whose house we are,” continuing the previous sentence.

6.anchor: if in fact we hold firmly
6.note: tc The reading adopted by the translation is found in Ì13,46 B sa, while the vast majority of mss (א A C D Ψ 0243 0278 33 1739 1881 Ï latt) add μέχρι τέλους βεβαίαν (mecri telou" bebaian, “secure until the end”). The external evidence for the omission, though minimal, has excellent credentials. Considering the internal factors, B. M. Metzger (TCGNT 595) finds it surprising that the feminine adjective βεβαίαν should modify the neuter noun καύχημα (kauchma, here translated “we take pride”), a fact that suggests that even the form of the word was borrowed from another place. Since the same phrase occurs at Heb 3:14, it is likely that later scribes added it here at Heb 3:6 in anticipation of Heb 3:14. While these words belong at 3:14, they seem foreign to 3:6.

6.anchor: to our confidence and the hope we take pride in.
6.note: tn Grk “the pride of our hope.”

7.anchor: that today you would listen as he speaks!
7.note: tn Grk “today if you hear his voice.”

10.note: tn Grk “they are wandering in the heart.”

11.note: tn Grk “if they shall enter my rest,” a Hebrew idiom expressing an oath that something will certainly not happen.

12.note: tn Or “take care.”
tn Grk “brothers.” See note on the phrase “brothers and sisters” in 2:11.
tn Grk “that there not be in any of you.”

12.anchor: an evil, unbelieving heart that forsakes
12.note: tn Or “deserts,” “rebels against.”
tn Grk “in forsaking the living God.”

14.anchor: For we have become partners with Christ, if in fact we hold our initial confidence
14.note: tn Grk “the beginning of the confidence.”

15.anchor: that today you would listen as he speaks!
15.note: tn Grk “today if you hear his voice.”

16.anchor: For which ones heard and rebelled? Was it not all who came out of Egypt under Moses’ leadership?

17.note: tn Grk “he”; in the translation the referent (God) has been specified for clarity.
sn An allusion to God’s judgment pronounced in Num 14:29, 32.

19.note: tn Here καί (kai) has been translated as “So” to indicate a summary or conclusion to the argument of the preceding paragraph.

1.anchor: Now the main point of what we are saying is this:
1.note: tn Grk “the main point of the things being said.”
sn An allusion to Ps 110:1; see Heb 1:3, 13.

4.anchor: Now if he were on earth, he would not be a priest, since there are already priests who offer
4.note: tn Grk “there are those who offer.”

5.anchor: The place where they serve is
5.note: tn Grk “who serve in,” referring to the Levitical priests, but focusing on the provisional and typological nature of the tabernacle in which they served.

tn Or “prototype,” “outline.” The Greek word ὑπόδειγμα (Jupodeigma) does not mean “copy,” as it is often translated; it means “something to be copied,” a basis for imitation. BDAG 1037 s.v. 2 lists both Heb 8:5 and 9:23 under the second category of usage, “an indication of someth. that appears at a subsequent time,” emphasizing the temporal progression between the earthly and heavenly sanctuaries.sn There are two main options for understanding the conceptual background of the heavenly sanctuary imagery. The first is to understand the imagery to be functioning on a vertical plane. This background is Hellenistic, philosophical, and spatial in orientation and sees the earthly sanctuary as a copy of the heavenly reality. The other option is to see the imagery functioning on a horizontal plane. This background is Jewish, eschatological, and temporal and sees the heavenly sanctuary as the fulfillment and true form of the earthly sanctuary which preceded it. The second option is preferred, both for lexical reasons (see tn above) and because it fits the Jewish context of the book (although many scholars prefer to emphasize the relationship the book has to Hellenistic thought).

5.anchor: See that you make everything according to the design
5.note: tn The word τύπος (tupos) here has the meaning “an archetype serving as a model, type, pattern, model” (BDAG 1020 s.v. 6.a). This is in keeping with the horizontal imagery accepted for this verse (see sn on “sketch” earlier in the verse). Here Moses was shown the future heavenly sanctuary which, though it did not yet exist, became the outline for the earthly sanctuary.
sn A quotation from Exod 25:40.

6.anchor: But
6.note: sn The Greek text indicates a contrast between vv. 4-5 and v. 6 that is difficult to render in English: Jesus’ status in the old order of priests (vv. 4-5) versus his superior ministry (v. 6).

6.anchor: now Jesus
6.note: tn Grk “he”; in the translation the referent (Jesus) has been specified for clarity.

6.anchor: has obtained a superior ministry, since
6.note: tn Grk “to the degree that.”

6.anchor: the covenant that he mediates is also better and is enacted
6.note: tn Grk “which is enacted.”

6.anchor: on better promises.
6.note: sn This linkage of the change in priesthood with a change in the law or the covenant goes back to Heb 7:12, 22 and is picked up again in Heb 9:6-15 and 10:1-18.

7.anchor: For if that first covenant had been faultless, no one would have looked for a second one.
7.note: tn Grk “no occasion for a second one would have been sought.”

8.note: tn Grk “for,” but providing an explanation of the God-intended limitation of the first covenant from v. 7.

sn The “fault” or limitation in the first covenant was not in its inherent righteousness, but in its design from God himself. It was never intended to be his final revelation or provision for mankind; it was provisional, always pointing toward the fulfillment to come in Christ.
tn Grk “he”; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

tc ‡ Several witnesses (א* A D* I K P Ψ 33 81 326 365 1505 2464 al latt co Cyr) have αὐτούς (autous) here, “[in finding fault with] them, [he says],” alluding to Israel’s failings mentioned in v. 9b. (The verb μέμφομαι [memfomai, “to find fault with”] can take an accusative or dative direct object.) The reading behind the text above (αὐτοίς, autoi"), supported by Ì46 א2 B D2 0278 1739 1881 Ï, is perhaps a harder reading theologically, and is more ambiguous in meaning. If αὐτοίς goes with μεμφόμενος (memfomeno", here translated “showing its fault”), the clause could be translated “in finding fault with them” or “in showing [its] faults to them.” If αὐτοίς goes with the following λέγει (legei, “he says”), the clause is best translated, “in finding/showing [its] faults, he says to them.” The accusative pronoun suffers no such ambiguity, for it must be the object of μεμφόμενος rather than λέγει. Although a decision is difficult, the dative form of the pronoun best explains the rise of the other reading and is thus more likely to be original.

9.anchor: It will not be like the covenant
9.note: tn Grk “not like the covenant,” continuing the description of v. 8b.

10.note: tn Grk “I will be to them for a God and they will be to me for a people,” following the Hebrew constructions of Jer 31.

11.anchor: And there will be no need at all
11.note: tn Grk “they will not teach, each one his fellow citizen…” The Greek makes this negation emphatic: “they will certainly not teach.”
tn Grk “from the small to the great.”

12.note: sn A quotation from Jer 31:31-34.

13.anchor: When he speaks of a new covenant,
13.note: tn Grk “when he says, ‘new,’” (referring to the covenant).

13.anchor: he makes the first obsolete. Now what is growing obsolete and aging is about to disappear.

1.note: sn A series of quotations from Gen 14:17-19.

2.note: tn Grk “to whom,” continuing the description of Melchizedek. Because of the length and complexity of the Greek sentence, a new sentence was started here in the translation.
tn Or “a tenth part.”
sn A quotation from Gen 14:20.
tn Grk “first being interpreted,” describing Melchizedek.

4.anchor: But see how great he must be, if
Abraham the patriarch gave him a tithe

5.anchor: And those of the sons of Levi who receive the priestly office
5.note: tn Or “the priesthood.”

5.anchor: have authorization according to the law to collect a tithe from the people, that is, from their fellow countrymen,
5.note: tn Grk “from their brothers.” See BDAG 18-19 s.v. ἀδελφός 2.b.

5.anchor: although they too are descendants of Abraham.
5.note: tn Grk “have come from the loins of Abraham.”

6.anchor: But Melchizedek
6.note: tn Grk “the one”; in the translation the referent (Melchizedek) has been specified for clarity.

6.anchor: who does not share their ancestry
6.note: tn Grk “is not descended from them.”

6.anchor: collected a tithe
6.note: tn Or “a tenth part.”

6.anchor: from Abraham and blessed
6.note: sn The verbs “collected…and blessed” emphasize the continuing effect of the past actions, i.e., Melchizedek’s importance.

10.anchor: For he was still in his ancestor Abraham’s loins
10.note: tn Grk “in the loins of his father” (a reference to Abraham). The name “Abraham” has been repeated in the translation at this point (cf. v. 9) in order to clarify the referent (i.e., what ancestor was in view).sn The point of the phrase still in his ancestor’s loins is that Levi was as yet unborn, still in his ancestor Abraham’s body. Thus Levi participated in Abraham’s action when Abraham paid tithes to Melchizedek.

11.anchor: So if perfection had in fact been possible through the Levitical priesthood – for on that basis

12.anchor: For when the priesthood changes, a change in the law must come
12.note: tn Grk “of necessity a change in the law comes to pass.”

13.anchor: Yet the one these things are spoken about belongs to
a different tribe, and no one from that tribe

16.anchor: who has become a priest not by a legal regulation about physical descent
16.note: tn Grk “a law of a fleshly command.”

17.anchor: For here is the testimony about him:
17.note: tn Grk “for he/it is witnessed that.”
sn A quotation from Ps 110:4 (see Heb 5:6 and 6:20).

18.anchor: On the one hand a former command is set aside
18.note: tn Grk “the setting aside of a former command comes to pass.”

18.anchor: because it is weak and useless,
18.note: tn Grk “because of its weakness and uselessness.”

20.note: sn The Greek text contains an elaborate comparison between v. 20a and v. 22, with a parenthesis (vv. 20b-21) in between; the comparison is literally, “by as much as…by so much” or “to the degree that…to that same degree.”

21.note: tn Grk “he”; the referent (Jesus) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
tn The words “did so” are not in the Greek text, but are implied.
sn A quotation from Ps 110:4 (see Heb 5:6, 6:20, and 7:17).

22.anchor: accordingly Jesus has become the guarantee

23.note: tn Grk “they on the one hand” in contrast with “he on the other hand” in v. 24.

23.anchor: who became priests were numerous, because death prevented them
23.note: tn Grk “they were prevented by death.”
tn Grk “from continuing” (the words “in office” are supplied for clarity).

28.anchor: For the law appoints as high priests men subject to weakness,
28.note: sn See Heb 5:2 where this concept was introduced.

1.note: tn Or “spoke formerly.”

tn Or “parts.” The idea is that God’s previous revelation came in many parts and was therefore fragmentary or partial (L&N 63.19), in comparison with the final and complete revelation contained in God’s Son. However, some interpret πολυμερῶς (polumerw") in Heb 1:1 to mean “on many different occasions” and would thus translate “many times” (L&N 67.11). This is the option followed by the NIV: “at many times and in various ways.” Finally, this word is also understood to refer to the different manners in which something may be done, and would then be translated “in many different ways” (L&N 89.81). In this last case, the two words πολυμερῶς and πολυτρόπως (polutropw") mutually reinforce one another (“in many and various ways,” NRSV).
tn These two phrases are emphasized in Greek by being placed at the beginning of the sentence and by alliteration.
tn Grk “to the fathers.”

2.anchor: in these last days he has spoken to us in a son,
2.note: tn The Greek puts an emphasis on the quality of God’s final revelation. As such, it is more than an indefinite notion (“a son”) though less than a definite one (“the son”), for this final revelation is not just through any son of God, nor is the emphasis specifically on the person himself. Rather, the focus here is on the nature of the vehicle of God’s revelation: He is no mere spokesman (or prophet) for God, nor is he merely a heavenly messenger (or angel); instead, this final revelation comes through one who is intimately acquainted with the heavenly Father in a way that only a family member could be. There is, however, no exact equivalent in English (“in son” is hardly good English style).sn The phrase in a son is the fulcrum of Heb 1:1-4. It concludes the contrast of God’s old and new revelation and introduces a series of seven descriptions of the Son. These descriptions show why he is the ultimate revelation of God.

2.anchor: whom he appointed heir of all things, and through whom he created the world.
2.note: tn Grk “the ages.” The temporal (ages) came to be used of the spatial (what exists in those time periods). See Heb 11:3 for the same usage.

3.note: tn Grk “who being…and sustaining.” Heb 1:1-4 form one skillfully composed sentence in Greek, but it must be broken into shorter segments to correspond to contemporary English usage, which does not allow for sentences of this length and complexity.

3.anchor: the radiance of his glory and the representation of his essence, and he sustains all things by his powerful word,
3.note: tn Grk “by the word of his power.”
sn An allusion to Ps 110:1, quoted often in Hebrews.

4.note: tn Grk “having become.” This is part of the same sentence that extends from v. 1 through v. 4 in the Greek text.

4.anchor: so far better than the angels as
4.note: tn Most modern English translations attempt to make the comparison somewhat smoother by treating “name” as if it were the subject of the second element: “as the name he has inherited is superior to theirs” (cf. NAB, NIV, NRSV, CEV). However, the Son is the subject of both the first and second elements: “he became so far better”; “he has inherited a name.” The present translation maintains this parallelism even though it results in a somewhat more awkward rendering.sn This comparison is somewhat awkward to express in English, but it reflects an important element in the argument of Hebrews: the superiority of Jesus Christ.

5.anchor: For to which of the angels did God
5.note: tn Grk “he”; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
tn Grk “I have begotten you.”sn A quotation from Ps 2:7.
tn Grk “And again,” quoting another OT passage.

tn The words “he says” are not in the Greek text but are supplied to make a complete English sentence. In the Greek text this is a continuation of the previous sentence, but English does not normally employ such long and complex sentences.
tn Grk “I will be a father to him and he will be a son to me.”sn A quotation from 2 Sam 7:14 (cf. 1 Chr 17:13).

6.anchor: But when he again brings
6.note: tn Or “And again when he brings.” The translation adopted in the text looks forward to Christ’s second coming to earth. Some take “again” to introduce the quotation (as in 1:5) and understand this as Christ’s first coming, but this view does not fit well with Heb 2:7. Others understand it as his exaltation/ascension to heaven, but this takes the phrase “into the world” in an unlikely way.
sn A quotation combining themes from Deut 32:43 and Ps 97:7.

7.note: sn The Greek correlative conjunctions μέν and δέ (men and de) emphasize the contrastive parallelism of vs. 7 (what God says about the angels) over against vv. 8-9 and vv. 10-12 (what God says about the son).
tn Grk “He who makes.”
sn A quotation from Ps 104:4.

8.note: tn Grk “the righteous scepter,” but used generically.

9.anchor: has anointed you over your companions
9.note: sn God…has anointed you over your companions. God’s anointing gives the son a superior position and authority over his fellows.

10.note: sn You founded the earth…your years will never run out. In its original setting Ps 102:25-27 refers to the work of God in creation, but here in Hebrews 1:10-12 the writer employs it in reference to Christ, the Lord, making a strong argument for the essential deity of the Son.

12.note: sn A quotation from Ps 102:25-27.

13.anchor: But to which of the angels
13.note: sn The parallel phrases to which of the angels in vv. 5 and 13 show the unity of this series of quotations (vv. 5-14) in revealing the superiority of the Son over angels (v. 4).

14.anchor: Are they not all ministering spirits, sent out to serve those
14.note: tn Grk “sent for service for the sake of those.”

2.anchor: Do not neglect hospitality, because through it some have entertained angels without knowing it.
2.note: sn This is a vague allusion to people described in scripture and extra-biblical literature and may include Abraham and Sarah (Gen 18:2-15), Lot (Gen 19:1-14), Gideon (Judg 6:11-18), Manoah (Judg 13:3-22), and possibly Tobit (Tob 12:1-20).

3.anchor: Remember those in prison as though you were in prison with them,
and those ill-treated as though you too felt their torment.
3.note: tn Or “since you too are vulnerable”; Grk “you also being in the body.”

5.note: sn A quotation from Deut 31:6, 8.

6.anchor: and
6.note: tc Some important mss (א* C* P 0285vid 33 1175 1739 pc lat) lack καί (kai), but because the omission conforms to the wording of Ps 118:6 (117:6 LXX), it is suspect.
sn A quotation from Ps 118:6.

9.anchor: Do not be carried away by all sorts of strange teachings.
9.note: tn Grk “by diverse and strange teachings.”

9.anchor: For it is good for the heart to be strengthened by grace, not ritual meals,
9.note: tn Grk “foods,” referring to the meals associated with the OT sacrifices (see the contrast with the next verse; also 9:9-10; 10:1, 4, 11).

11.anchor: For the bodies of those animals whose blood the high priest brings
11.note: tn Grk “whose blood is brought by the high priest.”

13.anchor: We must go out to him, then, outside the camp, bearing the abuse he experienced.

16.anchor: And do not neglect to do good and to share what you have,
16.note: tn Grk “neglect doing good and fellowship.”

17.anchor: Obey your leaders and submit to them, for they keep watch over your souls and will give an account for their work.
17.note: tn Or “as ones who will give an account”; Grk “as giving an account.”
tn Grk “that they may do this.”

19.anchor: I especially ask you to pray
19.note: tn Grk “I urge you to do this all the more.”

21.anchor: equip you with every good thing to do his will, working in us
21.note: tc Some mss (C P Ψ 6 629* 630 1505 pm latt syh) read ὑμῖν (Jumin, “in you”) here, but ἡμῖν (Jhmin) has stronger external support (Ì46 א A Dvid K 0243 0285 33 81 104 326 365 629c 1175 1739 1881 pm syp co). It is also more likely that ἡμῖν would have been changed to ὑμῖν in light of the “you” which occurs at the beginning of the verse than vice versa.

21.anchor: what is pleasing before him through Jesus Christ, to whom be glory forever.
21.note: tc ‡ Most mss (א A [C*] 0243 0285 33 1739 1881 Ï latt) include the words “and ever” here, but the shorter reading (supported by Ì46 C3 D Ψ 6 104 365 1505 al) is preferred on internal grounds. It seemed more likely that scribes would assimilate the wording to the common NT doxological expression “for ever and ever,” found especially in the Apocalypse (cf., e.g., 1 Tim 1:17; 2 Tim 4:18; Rev 4:9; 22:5) than to the “forever” of Heb 13:8. Nevertheless, a decision is difficult here. NA27 places the phrase in brackets, indicating doubts as to its authenticity.

22.anchor: Now I urge you, brothers and sisters,
22.note: tn Grk “brothers.” See note on the phrase “brothers and sisters” in 2:11.

23.anchor: our brother Timothy has been released. If he comes soon, he will be with me when I see you.
23.note: tn Grk “has been released, with whom, if he comes soon, I will see you.”

25.note: tc Most witnesses, including several important ones (א2 A C D H Ψ 0243 1739 1881 Ï lat sy bo), conclude the letter with ἀμήν (amhn, “amen”). Such a conclusion is routinely added by scribes to NT books because a few of these books originally had such an ending (cf. Rom 16:27; Gal 6:18; Jude 25). A majority of Greek witnesses have the concluding ἀμήν in every NT book except Acts, James, and 3 John (and even in these books, ἀμήν is found in some witnesses). It is thus a predictable variant. Further, there is sufficient testimony (Ì46 א* Ivid 6 33 sa) for the lack of the particle, rendering its omission the preferred reading.

2.anchor: For by it the people of old
2.note: tn Or “the elders,” “the ancients.”
tn Grk “were attested,” “received commendation”; and Heb 11:4-6 shows this to be from God.

3.anchor: By faith we understand that the worlds
3.note: tn Grk “ages.” The temporal (ages) came to be used of the spatial (what exists in those time periods). See Heb 1:2 for same usage.

3.anchor: were set in order at God’s command,
so that the visible has its origin in the invisible.
3.note: sn The Greek phrasing emphasizes this point by negating the opposite: “so that what is seen did not come into being from things that are visible.”

4.anchor: By faith Abel offered God a greater sacrifice than Cain, and through his faith
4.note: tn Or “through his sacrifice”; Grk “through which.”

4.anchor: he was commended as righteous, because God commended him for his offerings. And through his faith
4.note: tn Or “through his sacrifice”; Grk “through it.”

7.anchor: By faith Noah, when he was warned about things not yet seen, with reverent regard
7.note: tn Cf. BDAG 407 s.v. εὐλαβέομαι 2, “out of reverent regard (for God’s command).”

9.anchor: By faith he lived as a foreigner
9.note: tn Or “settled as a resident alien.”

9.anchor: in the promised land as though it were a foreign country, living in tents with Isaac and Jacob, who were fellow heirs

10.anchor: For he was looking forward to the city with firm foundations,

11.anchor: By faith, even though Sarah herself was barren and he was too old,
11.note: tn Grk “past the time of maturity.”

11.anchor: he received the ability to procreate,
11.note: tn Grk “power to deposit seed.” Though it is not as likely, some construe this phrase to mean “power to conceive seed,” making the whole verse about Sarah: “by faith, even though Sarah herself was barren and too old, she received ability to conceive, because she regarded the one who had given the promise to be trustworthy.”

12.note: tn Grk “these”; in the translation the referent (children) has been specified for clarity.

12.anchor: like the number of stars in the sky and like the innumerable grains of sand
12.note: tn Grk a collective “the sand.”
sn An allusion to Gen 22:17 (which itself goes back to Gen 15:5).

13.anchor: These all died in faith without receiving the things promised,
13.note: tn Grk “the promises,” referring to the things God promised, not to the pledges themselves.

13.anchor: but they saw them in the distance and welcomed them and acknowledged that they were strangers and foreigners

17.anchor: By faith Abraham, when he was tested, offered up Isaac. He had received the promises,
17.note: tn Here “received the promises” refers to the pledges themselves, not to the things God promised.

17.anchor: yet he was ready to offer up
17.note: tn Grk “he was offering up.” The tense of this verb indicates the attempt or readiness to sacrifice Isaac without the actual completion of the deed.

18.note: tn Grk “in Isaac seed will be named for you.”sn A quotation from Gen 21:12.

19.note: tn Grk “having reasoned,” continuing the ideas of v. 17.

19.anchor: that God could even raise him from the dead, and in a sense

21.note: tn Grk “worshiped on the top of his staff,” a quotation from Gen 47:31 (LXX).

22.anchor: By faith Joseph, at the end of his life,
mentioned the exodus of the sons of Israel
22.note: sn Joseph’s prophecy about the exodus of the sons of Israel is found in Gen 50:24.

22.anchor: and gave instructions about his burial.
22.note: tn Grk “about his bones,” which refers by metonymy to the disposition of his bones, i.e., his burial.sn The instructions about his burial are recorded in Gen 50:25.

23.anchor: By faith, when Moses was born, his parents hid him
23.note: tn Grk “Moses, when he was born, was hidden by his parents.”

26.anchor: He regarded abuse suffered for Christ
to be greater wealth than the treasures of Egypt, for his eyes were fixed on
26.note: tn Grk “he was looking away to.”

28.anchor: By faith he kept the Passover and the sprinkling of the blood,
28.note: tn Grk “the pouring out of the blood.”sn The sprinkling of the blood refers here to the application of the blood to the doorways of the Israelite houses (cf. Exod 12:7, 13).

30.anchor: By faith the walls of Jericho
30.note: map For location see Map5-B2; Map6-E1; Map7-E1; Map8-E3; Map10-A2; Map11-A1.

30.anchor: fell after the people marched around them
30.note: tn Grk “after they had been encircled.”

31.anchor: By faith Rahab the prostitute escaped the destruction of
31.note: tn Grk “did not perish together with.”

33.anchor: Through faith they conquered kingdoms, administered justice,
33.note: tn This probably refers to the righteous rule of David and others. But it could be more general and mean “did what was righteous.”

tn Grk “obtained promises,” referring to the things God promised, not to the pledges themselves. sn Gained what was promised. They saw some of God’s promises fulfilled, even though the central promise remained unfulfilled until Christ came (cf. vv. 39-40).

34.note: tn Grk “quenched the power of fire.”

34.anchor: escaped the edge of the sword, gained strength in weakness,

35.anchor: and women received back their dead raised to life.
35.note: tn Grk “received back their dead from resurrection.”

35.anchor: But others were tortured, not accepting release, to obtain resurrection to a better life.
35.note: tn Grk “to obtain a better resurrection.”

37.note: tc The reading ἐπρίσθησαν (ejprisqhsan, “they were sawed apart”) is found in some important witnesses (Ì46 [D* twice reads ἐπίρσθησαν, “they were burned”?] pc syp sa Orpt Eus). Other mss have ἐπειράσθησαν (ejpeirasqhsan, “they were tempted”), either before “sawed apart” ([א] L P [048] 33 81 326 1505 pc syh), after “sawed apart” (Ì13vid A D1 Ψ 1739 1881 Ï lat bo Orpt), or altogether in place of “sawed apart” (0150 vgmss Cl). Since the two words ἐπρίσθησαν and ἐπειράσθησαν are so much alike in sight and sound, and since the position of “they were tempted” varies in the mss, it seems best to say that ἐπειράσθησαν is an accidental corruption of ἐπρίσθησαν or an intentional change to a more common word (the root of ἐπρίσθησαν [πρίζω, prizw] occurs only here in the NT, while the root of ἐπειράσθησαν [πειράζω, peirazw] occurs 38 times). The best reading here seems to be “sawed apart” without any addition before or after. (See TCGNT 603-4, for a discussion of emendations that scholars have proposed for this difficult problem.)

39.note: sn The expression these all were commended forms an inclusio with Heb 11:2: The chapter begins and ends with references to commendation for faith.

39.anchor: for their faith, yet they did not receive what was promised.
39.note: tn Grk “the promise,” referring to the thing God promised, not to the pledge itself.

40.anchor: For God had provided something better for us, so that they would be made perfect together with us.
40.note: tn The Greek phrasing emphasizes this point by negating the opposite: “so that they would not be made perfect without us.”

1.anchor: Therefore, since we are surrounded by such a great cloud of witnesses,
1.note: tn Grk “having such a great cloud of witnesses surrounding us.”

2.note: sn An allusion to Ps 110:1.

4.anchor: You have not yet resisted to the point of bloodshed

5.anchor: or give up when he corrects
5.note: tn Or “reproves,” “rebukes.” The Greek verb ἐλέγχω (elencw) implies exposing someone’s sin in order to bring correction.

6.note: sn A quotation from Prov 3:11-12.

7.note: tn Grk “endure,” with the object (“your suffering”) understood from the context.
tn Or “in order to become disciplined.”

8.anchor: But if you do not experience discipline,

8.note: tn Grk “all”; “sons” is implied by the context.

9.anchor: Besides, we have experienced discipline from
9.note: tn Grk “we had our earthly fathers as discipliners.”

tn Grk “the fathers of our flesh.” In Hebrews, “flesh” is a characteristic way of speaking about outward, physical, earthly life (cf. Heb 5:7; 9:10, 13), as opposed to the inward or spiritual dimensions of life.

9.anchor: and we respected them; shall we not submit ourselves all the more to the Father of spirits and receive life?
9.note: tn Grk “and live.” sn Submit ourselves…to the Father of spirits and receive life. This idea is drawn from Proverbs, where the Lord’s discipline brings life, while resistance to it leads to death (cf. Prov 4:13; 6:23; 10:17; 16:17).

11.anchor: Now all discipline seems painful at the time, not joyful.
11.note: tn Grk “all discipline at the time does not seem to be of joy, but of sorrow.”

11.anchor: But later it produces the fruit of peace and righteousness
11.note: tn Grk “the peaceful fruit of righteousness.”

12.note: tn Or “straighten.”
sn A quotation from Isa 35:3. Strengthen your listless hands and your weak knees refers to the readers’ need for renewed resolve and fresh strength in their struggles (cf. Heb 10:36-39; 12:1-3).

13.note: sn A quotation from Prov 4:26. The phrase make straight paths for your feet is figurative for “stay on God’s paths.”

14.anchor: Pursue peace with everyone, and holiness,
14.note: sn The references to peace and holiness show the close connection between this paragraph and the previous one. The pathway toward “holiness” and the need for it is cited in Heb 12:10 and 14. More importantly Prov 4:26-27 sets up the transition from one paragraph to the next: It urges people to stay on godly paths (Prov 4:26, quoted here in v. 13) and promises that God will lead them in peace if they do so (Prov 4:27 [LXX], quoted in v. 14).

15.note: tn Grk “that there not be any root of bitterness,” but referring figuratively to a person who causes trouble (as in Deut 29:17 [LXX] from which this is quoted).sn An allusion to Deut 29:18.

16.anchor: And see to it that no one becomes
16.note: tn Grk “that there not be any,” continuing from v. 15.

17.note: tn Or a command: “for understand that.”

17.anchor: later when he wanted to inherit the blessing, he was rejected, for he found no opportunity for repentance, although he sought the blessing
17.note: tn Grk “it,” referring either to the repentance or the blessing. But the account in Gen 27:34-41 (which the author appeals to here) makes it clear that the blessing is what Esau sought. Thus in the translation the referent (the blessing) is specified for clarity.

18.anchor: For you have not come to something that can be touched,
18.note: tn This describes the nation of Israel approaching God on Mt. Sinai (Exod 19). There is a clear contrast with the reference to Mount Zion in v. 22, so this could be translated “a mountain that can be touched.” But the word “mountain” does not occur here and the more vague description seems to be deliberate.

19.anchor: and the blast of a trumpet and a voice uttering words
such that those who heard begged to hear no more.
19.note: tn Grk “a voice…from which those who heard begged that a word not be added to them.”

21.note: tn Grk “I am terrified and trembling.”sn A quotation from Deut 9:19.

22.anchor: But you have come to Mount Zion, the city
22.note: tn Grk “and the city”; the conjunction is omitted in translation since it seems to be functioning epexegetically – that is, explaining further what is meant by “Mount Zion.”

24.note: tn The Greek word μεσίτης (mesith", “mediator”) in this context does not imply that Jesus was a mediator in the contemporary sense of the word, i.e., he worked for compromise between opposing parties. Here the term describes his function as the one who was used by God to enact a new covenant which established a new relationship between God and his people, but entirely on God’s terms.

24.anchor: of a new covenant, and to the sprinkled blood that speaks of something better than Abel’s does.
24.note: sn Abel’s shed blood cried out to the Lord for justice and judgment, but Jesus’ blood speaks of redemption and forgiveness, something better than Abel’s does (Gen 4:10; Heb 9:11-14; 11:4).
